Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

G. E. M. Anscombe

1919 – 2001

Intention, action, and an anti-Cartesian grammar of thought and consciousness.

Anscombe did not leave a single systematic treatise called Philosophy of Mind. What she left is more interesting and less easily domesticated: a set of interventions on sensation, intentionality, first-person thought, mental events, action, practical knowledge, ethics, and soul-language, together with Catholic and Aristotelian commitments that are not ornamental but architectonic. The relevant corpus includes Intention, the mind-focused papers collected in Metaphysics and the Philosophy of Mind, and later essays such as “Analytical Philosophy and the Spirituality of Man,” “Has Mankind One Soul?,” “Human Essence,” “Were You a Zygote?,” “The Immortality of the Soul,” and “Knowledge and Reverence for Human Life.” The Metaphysics and the Philosophy of Mind volume itself contains “The Intentionality of Sensation,” “The First Person,” “Substance,” “The Subjectivity of Sensation,” “Events in the Mind,” “On Sensations of Position,” “Intention,” and related essays, so the title of the collection is not accidental.

The short reconstruction is this: Anscombe’s philosophy of mind is an anti-Cartesian, anti-phenomenalist, non-reductionist, Aristotelian-Wittgensteinian account of minded human life. The subject of thought, perception, intention, and action is not an immaterial ego, a private inner theater, or a brain-state complex, but the living human being. Yet this is not behaviorism or crude physicalism. Anscombe’s human being is an animal whose life is rational, linguistic, institutionally articulated, morally answerable, and—on her theological and Aristotelian view—spiritual. “Soul” names the principle or form of life of a living thing; “mind” is not a private object or inner organ; “consciousness,” when treated as a single metaphysical medium containing pains, thoughts, images, volitions, and sensations, is largely a Cartesian distortion.

That makes her philosophy of mind difficult to classify by contemporary options. It is not Cartesian dualism, not mind-brain identity theory, not functionalism, not behaviorism, not phenomenological sense-datum theory, and not the currently fashionable “consciousness-first” metaphysics. It is closer to what one might call an embodied, action-centered, grammatical realism about human mindedness. It studies the concepts by which mental life is intelligible: seeing, intending, thinking, believing, wanting, knowing, remembering, feeling pain, acting for a reason, and saying “I.”

The theological material matters because Anscombe’s resistance to a Cartesian “thinking substance” is not a secularizing move away from soul-talk. On the contrary, she thinks Cartesianism itself corrupts older soul-talk by transforming the soul into a private mental object. Her theological remarks push in a different direction: toward resurrection, human life, dignity, rationality, practical reason, and the spiritual orientation of human beings toward truth and goodness. A review by Alasdair MacIntyre of Faith in a Hard Ground is useful here because it explicitly notes that Anscombe’s Catholic beliefs and philosophical inquiries illuminate one another, rather than belonging to sealed-off domains.

1. The enemy: Cartesian “consciousness” as a false unifier

Anscombe’s philosophy of mind begins from a diagnosis: modern philosophy inherited from Descartes a destructive way of collecting many different psychological phenomena under one heading. In “Events in the Mind,” she says Descartes broadened cogitare or penser so that toothache, phantom-limb pain, sensations, understanding, willing, imagining, and other phenomena all become “thought” in the sense of something present to consciousness. The result is the modern notion of consciousness or experience as an inner domain containing everything mental.

This point is sharpened by Anscombe and Geach’s translation of Descartes. Haldane notes that they deliberately render Cartesian terms such as cogitare and res cogitans in ways that bring out the breadth of Descartes’s usage: not just intellectual activity, but willing, imagining, sensing, and undergoing passions. Descartes departs from the Aristotelian-medieval taxonomy by putting a heterogeneous range of psychological states into a single “mind” category, with consciousness as the criterion of inclusion.

Anscombe’s complaint is not merely historical. She thinks this Cartesian move distorts actual psychological grammar. Pain, belief, intention, image, sensation, thought, desire, understanding, and knowledge do not all have the same logical structure. Some are experiences; some are standing capacities or states; some are events but not experiences; some are intelligible only through the object, proposition, act, or practice to which they are internally related. To unify them as “items in consciousness” is already to have made a metaphysical mistake.

This is why Anscombe’s view can look deflationary about consciousness. She does not deny pain, experience, sensory appearance, or first-person avowal. She denies that “consciousness” names a common inner stuff, container, or arena that explains them. The proper method is not to ask, “What is the ontological nature of consciousness?” and then fit all psychological verbs into the answer. The proper method is to examine how these verbs function, what kinds of objects or contents they take, how first-person and third-person uses differ, and how they are embedded in action, language, and life.

The French reception captures this well. Aucouturier describes Anscombe’s philosophy of mind as central to her work, despite her greater fame as a philosopher of action, and emphasizes that her anti-Cartesianism is not a naturalistic reduction of mind to body. Rather, she contests the very framing of the mind-body problem as a confrontation between two substances or two domains.

2. Anscombe’s anti-phenomenalism: Wittgenstein’s therapy against private objects

Anscombe’s early philosophical formation is crucial. In the introduction to Metaphysics and the Philosophy of Mind, she recounts how Wittgenstein freed her from phenomenalism. She had been tempted by a view on which “yellow” or “pain” would be anchored in a private present item; Wittgenstein’s teaching dissolved the temptation to treat one’s experience as a private ostensive basis for public concepts.

The target is not simply “sense-data theory” as one doctrine among others. It is a whole picture of mind: the idea that genuine epistemic contact begins with private, immediately known objects—visual impressions, pain-data, color-data, inner images—and that the external world is constructed, inferred, or interpreted from them. Anscombe’s mature philosophy repeatedly attacks this picture, but she does not replace it with a simple-minded “ordinary language realism” that says we just see tables, chairs, and colors and that is all there is to say.

In “The Intentionality of Sensation,” she argues that both the sense-datum theorist and a certain ordinary-language direct realist are wrong. The sense-datum theorist misconstrues intentional objects as if they were material objects of a peculiar private kind; the ordinary-language realist, by contrast, fails to account for the intentional structure of perception and the possibility of non-material, indeterminate, erroneous, or merely apparent objects of sensory presentation.

Her key move is to describe perception verbs as intentional, or at least as having an essential intentional aspect. If I hallucinate a man, see a blur, seem to see something red, or feel pain in a foot that is no longer there, there is an object-description involved, but it does not follow that there is a material object answering to that description. Nor does it follow that there is a private mental object. The intentional object of an experience is not, as such, an entity whose ontological status must be settled before the experience can be described.

This is one of the deepest points in Anscombe’s philosophy of mind. She gives us a way to reject both naïve object realism about perception and inner-object theories of sensation. To have a visual impression as of a red patch is not to encounter a private red object. To have pain in a missing foot is not to locate pain in a ghost-foot, nor to reduce the pain to a brain event under a neurological description. It is to have an experience whose intentional description includes the foot. The “object” in such cases is given by the grammar of sensation, not by a metaphysical inventory of things.

Her treatment of “looking red” is especially important. She resists the idea that a child first grasps private red appearances and then learns to associate them with public red objects. On the contrary, the distinction between being red and looking red presupposes a wider mastery of ordinary color concepts, correction, appearance, and circumstance. The appearance-concept is not epistemologically prior to the object-concept.

3. The first person: “I” is not the name of an inner subject

Anscombe’s essay “The First Person” is one of the most difficult and radical texts in twentieth-century philosophy of mind. Its central negative thesis is often summarized as: “I” is not a name. More exactly, Anscombe argues that the first-person pronoun does not refer by naming an object known privately to the speaker. There is no path from “I” to a special object called the self, ego, mind, or subject.

Her target is the Cartesian-Augustinian inference: I cannot doubt that I exist; therefore, the “I” whose existence is indubitable is not my body; therefore, it is a mind, soul, or thinking substance. Anscombe presents Descartes and Augustine as sharing the assumption that “I” or “mind” names something whose existence is immediately self-known. She rejects that assumption. The first-person character of self-consciousness does not introduce a special object of reference.

This has a striking consequence for Cartesian dualism. Anscombe argues that if the Cartesian argument proves that “I” am not my body, it equally threatens to prove that “I” am not Descartes, the human being. That is absurd. The problem lies in treating “I” as a referring expression whose referent has to be identified by some special epistemic route.

The philosophical illusion she wants to destroy is the invention of “the subject” as an extraordinary object: not the human being, not the body, not even the soul in any traditional sense, but something mysteriously involved in all thinking and sensing. Anscombe explicitly rejects this move. The first-person form of sensory and psychological reports does not introduce a thing; “I” is not a name for a private subject.

This is not the denial that there are people, nor the denial that a speaker can be the human being Elizabeth Anscombe. It is a denial that “I” works by picking out an object under an identifying description. The living human being speaks, thinks, acts, and suffers. But the first-person pronoun does not function like “Elizabeth Anscombe,” “this body,” “this soul,” or “this mind.”

Portuguese-language scholarship has been especially useful in resisting a shallow reading of this essay. Sofia Miguens notes that many commentators take Anscombe simply to be denying that “I” refers and then dismiss the paper, but an alternative line of interpretation—associated with figures such as Descombes, Longuenesse, and Narboux—sees the essay as a challenge to the Cartesian conception of self-knowledge and consciousness. On this reading, the paper is not a technical oddity in the philosophy of language; it is a central anti-Cartesian text in philosophy of mind.

4. Psychological verbs: first-person authority without inner observation

Anscombe’s account of self-knowledge is neither introspectionism nor skepticism. She accepts that first-person psychological utterances have a special status. “I have a headache,” “I want an apple,” “I believe him,” “I intend to go,” and “I remember that…” are not ordinarily based on observing evidence. But that does not mean they are reports of private objects inspected by an inner eye.

In “Events in the Mind,” she stresses the asymmetry between first-person and third-person uses of psychological verbs. If I say that someone else has a headache, wants an apple, or believes a story, I may rely on behavior, circumstances, testimony, or inference. If I say it of myself, the ordinary request for observational grounds often makes no sense. But the form of the answer differs from verb to verb. The grounds for “I believe him” are usually grounds for the truth of what he says, not inner signs of belief. The grounds for “I want that apple” may be reasons for wanting it, not introspected wanting-objects.

Her point is subtle. First-person authority is not a single epistemic mechanism called “introspection.” The first-person grammar of pain differs from that of belief; belief differs from intention; intention differs from image; image differs from memory; memory differs from bodily position; and so on. The Cartesian category of consciousness erases these differences by treating all as inner contents.

Anscombe therefore gives a pluralist account of mental discourse. At the end of “Events in the Mind,” she distinguishes three broad classes. First, there are experiences, such as sensations and images, which may be occurrences with contents. Second, there are phenomena such as intention, understanding, knowledge, and belief, which are not events and need not have their objects “before the mind.” Third, there are thoughts that p: these may be events, but they are not experiences in the Cartesian sense. Their content is given by words with a primary application in the world, not by a description of an inner episode.

This is one of the most important passages for reconstructing her philosophy of mind. She is not saying that thoughts never occur. She is saying that when a thought occurs—say, “the letters must be posted by 8:15”—the content of the thought is not a closer specification of an inner experience. The thought is about letters, posting, and time; it is not an internal mental item that happens to be accompanied by that verbal description.

Hence her memorable anti-Cartesian claim: one thing that is not a “thought” in Descartes’s sense is precisely a thought. Descartes’s category of cogitatio becomes too broad to capture the logical grammar of actual thinking.

5. Thought is not an inner process in a bodily or immaterial organ

Anscombe’s attack on Cartesianism is also an attack on a bad argument for immateriality. The argument says: thinking is not the activity of a bodily part; therefore, it must be the activity of an immaterial part. In “Analytical Philosophy and the Spirituality of Man,” Anscombe rejects this inference. The proper question is why we should not simply say that thinking is an activity of the human being who thinks. There is no need to locate thought in an inner part, whether material or immaterial.

This is not a reduction of thought to bodily motion. It is a refusal of the premise that every activity must be assigned to an organ-like part. Walking is done with legs; seeing with eyes; but calculating, promising, judging, intending, and meaning are not in the same way processes inside a machine. Some human activities have organs, instruments, and necessary bodily conditions without being activities of those organs alone.

This is also where Anscombe differs from a standard physicalist. If one says, “Thinking is just a brain process,” Anscombe would ask what fixes the content of the thought. A brain state described neurologically does not by itself specify that someone is thinking about banks, debts, clocks, justice, or God. Haldane notes that, for Anscombe, the content of belief is partly fixed by the world and by practices: a belief about banks presupposes a world in which banks, money, institutional roles, and relevant forms of life exist. Thoughts are not identifiable with brain states under purely internal physical descriptions.

This gives Anscombe an externalist and practice-dependent theory of thought. Meaning and content are not private accompaniments of behavior. They are also not reducible to neural configuration. They are embedded in language, institutions, forms of life, and patterns of action. This is why paying, promising, commanding, praying, calculating, confessing, or marrying cannot be analyzed as bodily movements plus inner mental episodes. They are acts intelligible only within human practices.

A Spanish translation of “Analytical Philosophy and the Spirituality of Man” makes this aspect vivid: after rejecting the move from “not bodily part” to “immaterial part,” Anscombe points toward action embedded in communal practices and toward thought as something not necessarily accompanied by a special inner process.

6. Perception and sensation: intentionality without sense-data

Anscombe’s theory of perception is a central part of her philosophy of mind because it generalizes the structure of intentionality beyond thought and action. Sensation is intentional, but not because every sensation is a judgment or representation in the modern cognitive-science sense. It is intentional because sensory descriptions often involve an object-as-presented, and that object may be indeterminate, absent, misdescribed, or only apparent.

The key negative point is this: from “someone sees something” it does not follow that there exists a private entity that is the thing seen. Anscombe compares this with grammar. “He worships Zeus” does not imply that Zeus exists. “He is looking for a unicorn” does not imply that a unicorn is there. Similarly, “he seems to see a red patch” does not require a private red patch existing in consciousness. The intentional object of a sensation is not a material object; but neither is it an immaterial mental object.

This undermines a huge amount of modern philosophy of perception. The sense-datum theorist says: when I hallucinate or am deceived, there must still be something immediately present to me, namely a sense-datum. Anscombe’s reply is that this mistakes the grammar of intentional description for an ontological commitment. There may be no such object, or the object may be indeterminate, without the sensory report becoming meaningless.

She also criticizes a certain “ordinary language” realism because it refuses to acknowledge these intentional structures. It says we only see material objects, and so it lacks adequate language for hallucination, illusion, appearance, and non-veridical experience. Anscombe’s position is therefore not simply “we directly perceive the external world.” It is: perception verbs have intentional grammar, and any adequate realism must account for that grammar without inventing private objects.

This gives us a distinctive Anscombean answer to the problem of perceptual consciousness. We should not begin with “qualia” as inner atoms of consciousness. We should begin with what it is to see, seem to see, look, appear, feel, be struck by, mistake, recognize, and describe. The phenomenological richness of perception is real, but it is not a private mental theater.

7. Bodily awareness: knowledge without observation, and without clues

Anscombe’s account of bodily awareness is just as important as her account of visual perception. In “On Sensations of Position,” she connects bodily self-knowledge with the famous theme from Intention: knowledge without observation. She later clarifies that this should also mean knowledge without clues.

Suppose I know that my legs are crossed. On an ordinary occasion, I do not infer this from sensations of pressure, resistance, or weight. Nor do I observe my legs visually and then conclude their position. There may be sensations causally necessary for this knowledge; for instance, an anaesthetized limb may disrupt one’s awareness. But causal necessity does not make those sensations evidential clues.

Anscombe is fighting the same inner-object temptation. We are tempted to posit a bodily-position datum: an inner sensation from which I infer the posture of my body. But she argues that “my leg is bent,” “it feels as if my leg is bent,” and “I have a sensation of my leg’s being bent” can, in the right contexts, collapse into overlapping forms of expression rather than distinct observational stages. Where my leg is in fact bent, the ordinary case is simply that I know its position straight off.

This matters for philosophy of mind because it gives an alternative to two familiar models. On the Cartesian model, bodily awareness is inner awareness of private bodily sensations. On a detached third-person physicalist model, bodily awareness is a representation generated by neural proprioceptive systems. Anscombe’s approach begins from the embodied agent: the living person who moves, stands, reaches, walks, and acts intentionally. Bodily self-knowledge is part of agency, not a theoretical inference from internal data.

8. Intention and action: mind is visible in what agents do

For Anscombe, the philosophy of mind cannot be separated from the philosophy of action. This is not because action is outward behavior caused by inner mental states. It is because many central mental concepts—intention, wanting, trying, choosing, knowing what one is doing, acting for a reason—are practical concepts. They are displayed in the order of human action itself.

Intention is therefore a philosophy-of-mind text as much as an action-theory text. Anscombe’s famous question is: under what descriptions is an action intentional? One and the same bodily movement may be intentional under one description and not under another. A person may intentionally move an arm, intentionally pump water, unintentionally poison inhabitants, and intentionally replenish a house, depending on knowledge, reasons, descriptions, and context. The issue is not which inner mental event caused the movement; the issue is how the act is intelligible under descriptions.

The central diagnostic device is the “Why?” question. Intentional actions are those to which a certain sense of “Why?” applies. The relevant answer gives a reason for acting, though Anscombe insists that “reason” itself needs philosophical elucidation and should not be confused with an inner cause.

This is where Anscombe resists what later became the Davidsonian causal theory of action: the idea that intentional action is bodily movement caused in the right way by beliefs and desires. She does not deny that actions have causes. She denies that the intelligibility of action as intentional is captured by identifying mental antecedents that cause bodily events. Practical thought is not first an inner psychological episode and then secondarily an external movement. The practical order is internal to what is done.

SEP’s discussion of Anscombe’s action theory emphasizes that intentional action is known without observation and that practical knowledge is not an observational record of what one does. It is knowledge in accordance with which the action is performed. The famous contrast is between a shopping list and a detective’s record: the shopper’s list gives an order the world is to be made to fit; the detective’s list records what the shopper in fact buys.

This practical-knowledge thesis is not a side doctrine. It is Anscombe’s alternative to the inner-theater conception of mind. Intention is not a ghostly mental accompaniment to bodily movement. The agent knows what she is doing because her knowledge is practically operative in the doing. The mind is not hidden behind the action; in many cases, the mind is manifest in the action’s rational form.

The French, Portuguese, and Italian reception all stress this point. French discussions describe Anscombe as shifting attention from what is “in the head” to what agents do in context, while warning that this is not behaviorism. Brazilian material on Intention emphasizes that actions are organized in rational means-end order and situated in cultural and institutional contexts. Italian reception likewise stresses that intention is not a private inner state but is public through descriptions, reasons, and practical control.

9. The soul: Aristotelian form of life, not Cartesian ego

Anscombe’s soul-talk must be read against this whole background. “Soul” does not mean a private object of introspection. It does not mean the referent of “I.” It does not mean the Cartesian thinking substance. In the Aristotelian-Thomistic register she shares with Geach, soul is the form, organization, or primary principle of life of a living body. Haldane summarizes Anscombe as accepting that the primary principle of life of a material thing is its soul, and that in the human case the intellective principle is the specific differentia of the human soul.

This does not collapse human beings into plants or non-human animals. Anscombe accepts that vegetative and animal capacities are transposed in human life. Eating, perceiving, moving, desiring, reproducing, learning, speaking, promising, calculating, worshiping, and acting under moral descriptions are not separable layers pasted onto a neutral organism. They are dimensions of one human form of life. The human soul is not an added ghost; it is the rational form of the living human being.

This is why her view is best called Aristotelian rather than Cartesian. The Cartesian begins with consciousness and asks how it relates to body. Anscombe begins with the living human being and asks how various capacities—perception, appetite, reason, language, action, knowledge, morality—belong to that life. Mind is not a second substance alongside body; it is not even best understood as an inner realm. It is an aspect of the life of the rational animal.

At the same time, Anscombe is not a reductive naturalist. Haldane notes that she sees human life as possessing a specific non-materiality, visible in the irreducibility of calculating to mark-making or intending an object to extending a finger. But she resists reifying the intellectual principle into a separate intellectual subject. The thinking subject is the living human being, a kind of body, not a detached immaterial ego.

This lets us formulate her view with precision: the soul is not an object inside the body; the mind is not an object inside the soul; consciousness is not an object inside the mind; and “I” is not the name of any of these. The human being is ensouled as living; minded as rational, perceptive, linguistic, and practical; conscious in many distinct senses; and spiritual insofar as human life is ordered to truth, goodness, and transcendent norms.

10. Spirituality: not “immaterial stuff,” but rational transcendence

“Spirituality” in Anscombe is easy to misunderstand. She is not defending a vague spiritualism or a Cartesian immaterial substance. In “Analytical Philosophy and the Spirituality of Man,” she appears to be asking how analytic philosophy can acknowledge what is genuinely spiritual in human beings without falling into the bad metaphysics of an inner ghost.

The Spanish version of the essay is helpful because it explicitly frames the modern options as Cartesian dualism, brain-identity theory, and behaviorism, then points to Wittgenstein as breaking that opposition: human mindedness is neither a sequence of material events, nor an immaterial substance, nor mere behavior.

A recent French discussion, “Les animaux spirituels,” reads Anscombe as using spirit across several registers: thought or mind; human mental capacities such as imagination, desire, memory, perception, and intelligence; and, more broadly, sensitivity to value and transcendence. It emphasizes that the distinctiveness of spirit first appears in knowledge and in the radical difference displayed by thinking.

The Lumen Christi description of Anselm Müller’s lecture on Anscombe makes a similar point: human beings are essentially spiritual because they are naturally and consciously oriented beyond merely material life toward transcendent norms of truth and goodness. This is also why spirituality is connected to dignity: not because a human being contains a detachable spiritual object, but because the form of human life is answerable to truth, goodness, and moral law.

This is a crucial correction to common readings. Anscombe’s spirituality is not “qualia plus religion.” It is the form of life of rational animals: beings who can mean, judge, infer, promise, repent, intend, deliberate, worship, deceive, confess, calculate, know, and act under descriptions. The spiritual is not discovered by introspection into private consciousness. It is displayed in the public and practical life of language, action, knowledge, and morality.

11. Immortality and resurrection: theology against bad soul-metaphysics

Anscombe’s theological remarks on the immortality of the soul are among the most important materials for this topic. They prevent us from assimilating her Aristotelian soul-talk to a simple doctrine of a naturally immortal immaterial substance.

In “The Immortality of the Soul,” as summarized by MacIntyre’s review, Anscombe argues that the conception of the soul as an immortal substance is delusive. She denies that the immateriality of thought gives a basis for proving the soul’s spirituality, still less its immortality. She also denies that there is philosophical reason to believe in a temporal immortality of the soul apart from resurrection. The Christian doctrine, on this reading, is not that an immortal soul survives and resurrection is added later as an appendix; rather, it is unending human life after resurrection.

Haldane’s discussion gives the sharper formulation. Anscombe holds that, even if the principle of rational life in Elizabeth Anscombe is called her soul, that soul is not the reference of “I” and is not what she is. She is Elizabeth Anscombe, the human being, and she exists as that human being. Haldane also reports Anscombe’s view that Aquinas’s problem—how the soul can be the form of the body and yet survive death—is a real problem, and that it is not obvious that it has been solved.

This is theologically serious and philosophically austere. Anscombe does not use Christianity to smuggle Cartesian dualism back in. She uses Christian doctrine, especially resurrection, to block the fantasy that the real person is a naturally immortal inner substance. In that respect, her theology supports her anti-Cartesian philosophy of mind.

The consequence is that an Anscombean account of immortality must be personal and resurrectional, not ego-substantialist. The Christian hope is not that the inner referent of “I” floats free from bodily life. It is that the human being lives again. If there is a separated soul, it is not a complete human person in the ordinary sense, and it is not the object named by “I.”

12. Ethics requires philosophy of mind: intention, double effect, and human dignity

Anscombe’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from her moral philosophy. Her famous essay “Modern Moral Philosophy” argues that moral philosophy should be postponed until we have an adequate philosophy of psychology. That claim is not a rhetorical flourish. It reflects her view that modern moral theory often mishandles concepts such as intention, motive, desire, wanting, practical reason, pleasure, voluntariness, and action.

Her Catholic moral commitments intensify rather than distract from this problem. Consider double effect, killing, intention, and side effect. Whether an act is murder, self-defense, martyrdom, betrayal, euthanasia, or legitimate medical treatment depends on intentional descriptions, reasons, means, ends, and what the agent is doing—not merely on physical outcomes or private mental accompaniments. A bad philosophy of mind produces a bad moral psychology, and a bad moral psychology corrupts ethics.

This is why Anscombe’s attack on “intention in the head” matters. If intention were merely an inner mental item, then moral assessment would turn on hidden episodes rather than on the intelligible structure of action. Conversely, if intention were reduced to outward behavior, then the agent’s practical reasoning would disappear. Anscombe wants neither. Intention is internal to action under descriptions, answerable to the “Why?” question, and bound to practical knowledge.

Her writings on human life, embryos, dignity, and reverence for human life also belong here. The bibliography of Human Life, Action and Ethics includes essays on “Human Essence,” “Were You a Zygote?,” “Embryos and Final Causes,” “Knowledge and Reverence for Human Life,” and “The Dignity of the Human Being.” These are not peripheral to mind. They belong to a single view of the human being as a rational animal whose life has form, teleology, dignity, and moral significance.

A French discussion of “Knowledge and Reverence for Human Life” links Anscombe’s account of moral knowledge with connatural knowledge, the understanding of human action under multiple descriptions, and Thomistic themes about the gifts of the Holy Spirit. Even if one does not accept the theology, the interpretive point is right: her moral psychology is not detachable from her account of how human beings understand action, motive, description, and life.

13. Systematic extraction: twelve theses of an Anscombean philosophy of mind

Here is the most compact systematic reconstruction.

First, the subject of mental life is the living human being. The thinker, perceiver, intender, believer, rememberer, sufferer, and agent is not a mind behind the body, nor a body plus an attached mind, but the human being.

Second, “soul” is the principle or form of life, not a Cartesian ego. Human beings have rational souls in the Aristotelian sense: their life is organized by capacities for reason, speech, knowledge, action, morality, and spiritual orientation. But the soul is not the referent of “I.”

Third, “mind” is not an inner place. It is not a container for mental objects. To understand mind, we examine activities and capacities: thinking, perceiving, imagining, intending, calculating, judging, acting, speaking, promising, and knowing.

Fourth, “consciousness” is not a univocal metaphysical medium. The Cartesian grouping of pain, image, thought, will, sensation, belief, and understanding under “consciousness” obscures their different grammars.

Fifth, first-person authority is not inner observation. “I am in pain,” “I believe him,” “I intend to go,” and “I remember” have first-person uses not grounded in evidence, but this does not imply access to private mental objects.

Sixth, “I” is not a name. The first-person pronoun does not pick out a private subject, soul, ego, or mental substance. The speaker is the human being, but “I” does not function as an identifying name for that human being.

Seventh, sensation is intentional without being sense-datum-based. Experiences can have intentional objects that are absent, indeterminate, or merely apparent. This does not license private mental objects.

Eighth, thought is not an experience-object. A thought that p is not a private item whose content is described by p. The content is worldly, linguistic, and propositional; often it is embedded in practices and institutions.

Ninth, bodily awareness is agentive and non-observational. One often knows one’s bodily position or what one is doing without observing evidence or consulting inner clues.

Tenth, intention is practical, not merely psychological. Intention is understood through action under descriptions, reasons, means-end order, and practical knowledge, not through hidden mental causes.

Eleventh, spirituality is real but not ghostly. Human beings transcend merely material description through truth, goodness, knowledge, rational action, language, morality, worship, and dignity—not because they contain a detachable inner object.

Twelfth, theology corrects bad metaphysics. Christian resurrection doctrine, in Anscombe’s handling, resists the fantasy of a naturally immortal private ego. The hope is the life of the human being, not the career of an inner Cartesian soul.

14. Non-English reception and why it matters

The French reception is especially rich. Aucouturier’s work emphasizes that Anscombe’s philosophy of action and philosophy of mind must be read together, and that she resists the mind-body problem as framed by Cartesian mythology rather than solving it by reduction. Other French discussions stress that psychological concepts are learned in practices and forms of life, not by private ostension, and that Anscombe rejects the reification of mental events, sense-data, self, and substantial consciousness.

French sources also bring out a theme sometimes underplayed in Anglophone analytic reception: Anscombe’s “spiritual animal” anthropology. The recent “Les animaux spirituels” discussion treats spirit not as immaterial stuff but as the human orientation to knowledge, value, and transcendence. That is a valuable bridge between the philosophy of mind, moral philosophy, and theology.

The Portuguese and Brazilian reception is useful on two fronts. First, it highlights Intention as a text in philosophy of psychology, not only action theory. Brazilian material on the Portuguese translation stresses the “Why?” question, rational process, and action for an end. Second, Sofia Miguens’s discussion of “The First Person” resists the narrow reading of the paper as merely a thesis about reference and instead places it in debates over consciousness, self-knowledge, and the location of the mental in the objective world.

The Spanish reception makes visible Anscombe’s explicit confrontation with the modern menu of Cartesian dualism, brain-identity theory, and behaviorism. The Spanish materials around Filosofía analítica y la espiritualidad del hombre and “La intencionalidad de la sensación” are particularly relevant because they keep together the themes of spirituality, sensation, Descartes, Wittgenstein, and analytic method.

The German reception, including the Suhrkamp collection of Anscombe’s essays, shows that German-language readers are being given the metaphysics and philosophy-of-mind essays alongside the more famous action-theory material. The collection includes “Die erste Person” and “Die Intentionalität der Wahrnehmung,” confirming that these texts are central to Anscombe’s philosophical profile rather than specialist curiosities.

The Italian reception, especially discussions around Intenzione, emphasizes the relation between intention, practical agency, reasons, and the rejection of intention as a private inner state. One Italian discussion explicitly connects Anscombe’s philosophy of action to philosophy of language, philosophy of mind, and cognitive-science debates, while stressing the Aristotelian practical-syllogistic background.

Across these non-English materials, a pattern emerges: Anglophone reception often isolates Anscombe into action theory, “Modern Moral Philosophy,” or the first-person reference debate. The broader European and Latin American reception more often keeps together action, mind, language, anti-Cartesianism, spirituality, and ethics. That broader frame is closer to the systematic philosophy of mind one should extract from her work.

15. Tensions, hard questions, and interpretive cautions

There are real tensions in Anscombe’s view. The first concerns “I.” If “I” is not a name and does not refer to an object, how can we still say that the speaker is this human being? Anscombe’s answer is that ordinary first-person speech does not require a theory of reference to a private subject. But many philosophers have found this too austere. The more charitable reading is not that she denies persons, but that she denies the Cartesian semantics that makes the self into an object of privileged acquaintance.

The second tension concerns consciousness. Anscombe gives powerful reasons to reject “consciousness” as a Cartesian container. But contemporary philosophers of consciousness may object that she has not solved the “hard problem” of phenomenal character. An Anscombean reply would be that the hard problem inherits the very reifications she rejects: qualia, inner presentation, private objects, and the idea that experience must be metaphysically located before it can be described. Whether that dissolves or merely evades the contemporary problem remains contested.

The third tension concerns practical knowledge. Anscombe says intentional action is known without observation, but what about error, failure, clumsiness, surprise, and self-deception? Later action theorists debate whether practical knowledge is formal, efficient, normative, or constitutive of intentional action. The issue is not a minor technicality; it determines whether Anscombe’s philosophy of mind can fully explain the relation between intention and embodied execution.

The fourth tension concerns the soul after death. Anscombe’s anti-Cartesianism gives a strong reason to reject the naturally immortal ego, but Christian doctrine has historically included language about the soul’s survival between death and resurrection. Anscombe does not pretend this is easy. Her view, as reported by Haldane, is that Aquinas’s problem about the soul as form of the body yet somehow surviving death is genuinely difficult and not obviously solved.

The fifth tension concerns naturalism. Anscombe rejects Cartesian dualism and refuses to posit an immaterial thinking subject, but she also rejects reductive accounts of thought as brain process or behavior. Her position is therefore vulnerable to pressure from both sides. The dualist asks where the spiritual intellect is “located”; the physicalist asks what more there is than organism, brain, and behavior. Anscombe’s answer is that both questions may already impose the wrong grammar on human life.

Conclusion

Anscombe’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as a philosophy of ensouled, embodied, rational human life. Its negative center is anti-Cartesian: no private ego, no inner theater, no sense-data ontology, no consciousness-container, no reduction of thought to introspected experience, no treatment of intention as a hidden mental cause. Its positive center is Aristotelian and Wittgensteinian: the human being is a living rational animal whose mind is manifest in perception, language, action, practical knowledge, moral responsibility, and orientation to truth and goodness.

The soul, in this picture, is not a ghostly mind-substance. It is the form or principle of human life. Mind is not an inner object but the rational and perceptive life of the human being. Consciousness is not a single metaphysical stuff but a family of first-person, experiential, practical, perceptual, and cognitive phenomena whose grammars differ. Theology does not distract from this account. It deepens and disciplines it: especially by preventing the Cartesian deformation of the soul into a private immortal ego and by relocating Christian hope in resurrection and the life of the whole human being.

So the comprehensive Anscombean philosophy of mind is not a theory of “the mental” as a domain. It is a theory of persons as living human beings: animals with souls, bodies, language, reasons, actions, knowledge, institutions, moral accountability, and spiritual dignity.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
